title: The Null Pointer Murder
logline: Fix the crashed engine and find the saboteur.
educational_goals: Understand Servlet Container and Lifecycle management
Distinguish between Forward and Redirect mechanisms
Apply MVC patterns in web applications

characters.name: Alex Chen
characters.background: Expert in Java systems security investigating a production crash.
characters.secret: Previously made an initialization error years ago.
characters.goal: Identify who caused the lifecycle failure without losing certification.

characters.name: Ben Miller
characters.background: Developed the core service layer. Under pressure recently.
characters.secret: Skipped resource cleanup in destroy() to save time.
characters.goal: Protect his work history from being flagged as negligence.

characters.name: Cindy Luo
characters.background: Designed the app structure and routing logic.
characters.secret: Chose Redirect over Forward incorrectly, causing data loss.
characters.goal: Maintain reputation as the smartest architect.

scenes.title: The Dead Engine Room
scenes.description: Logs show repeated exceptions during startup. The container stopped accepting requests.

scenes.title: Debug Session
scenes.description: Code review session to trace the request path through controllers.

scenes.title: Architecture Reveal
scenes.description: Final decision on architectural responsibility for the crash.

clues.name: Startup Log
clues.description: Text file showing 'Container' initialized but instance did not call 'init().'
clues.reveals: The lifecycle initialization sequence was interrupted.

clues.name: Garbage Heap Dump
clues.description: Memory dump showing resources were never released.
clues.reveals: The 'destroy()' method failed to run properly.

clues.name: Thread Stack
clues.description: Stack trace indicating concurrent access issues.
clues.reveals: Multiple threads accessed the same service instance without lock.

clues.name: Controller Code
clues.description: Snippet showing 'doPost' calling a redirect instead of forward.

clues.name: JSP View File
clues.description: A view page that expects data missing from session scope.
clues.reveals: Data passed via forward was lost due to redirect.

clues.name: Request Header
clues.description: HTTP header shows new session ID created upon reload.
clues.reveals: Browser sent a second request, creating duplicate data.

clues.name: Design Doc
clues.description: Blueprint separating Model, View, and Controller components.
clues.reveals: Components were separated but coupled via RequestDispatcher.

clues.name: Bean Object
clues.description: Java class holding business logic independent of UI.
clues.reveals: Model logic was correct, but Controller misdirected it.

clues.name: Main Signature
clues.description: Class definition lacking a main() function inside Servlet.
clues.reveals: No main() required because Container handles execution.

questions.content: Who is responsible for managing the lifecycle of this software component?

hints.content: It has its own main program and loads classes automatically.

options.feedback: Correct. The Container manages loading, instantiation, and destruction.
Incorrect. Browsers send requests, but don't manage component life.

questions.content: Arrange the initial steps of a Servlet's life cycle:
questions.feedbackCorrect: Perfect order. Loading precedes instantiation, which precedes initialization.

questions.content: What happens in the init() method regarding configuration?

hints.content: This method runs once per instance lifetime.

options.content: Execute one-time setup like DB connections
options.feedback: Yes, init() is for one-time initialization tasks.
No. That is handled by service().

questions.content: Which method handles individual client requests?

options.feedback: Correct. It dispatches requests to doGet/doPost.
No, Servlets do not have a main() method.

questions.content: How does the container handle thread usage during service()?

hints.content: One thread per request for the same instance.

options.content: Creates a new thread for each request
options.feedback: Right. Concurrent access requires thread safety.

options.content: Runs on the main thread only
options.feedback: Wrong. This would block other users.

questions.content: What happens if destroy() is skipped before unloading?

options.feedback: Correct. Garbage collection follows later but leaks persist until then.
No. Connections hold up memory until explicit release.

questions.content: Which method receives GET requests in HttpServlet?

options.feedback: Yes. doGet handles retrieval operations.
Put is for updates, Get is for read.

questions.content: Which class provides HTTP-specific functionality?

options.feedback: Correct. It specializes in HTTP protocol.
Generic is for any protocol, HttpServlet is specifically HTTP.

questions.content: Which interface encapsulates browser request data?

options.feedback: Yes. Contains params, headers, cookies.
That sends data back, doesn't receive.

questions.content: How do you set content type for response?

options.feedback: Correct. Tells browser how to render.
Writer writes, SetType defines format.

questions.content: Is HttpServlet abstract or concrete?

options.feedback: Yes, must override methods like doGet.
No, you get compilation errors.

questions.content: Which object contains parameters from form submission?

options.feedback: Correct. It wraps incoming client data.
Context is global, not per-request param storage.

questions.content: Which object sends binary data like images back?

hints.content: Get output stream to write binary.

options.feedback: Yes. Use getOutputStream() or getWriter().
Request reads, Response writes.

questions.content: When does a Post request occur?

options.feedback: Correct. Used for inserting/updating info.
Images usually retrieved via GET.

questions.content: What mechanism allows internal server request sharing?

options.feedback: Yes. One request processed internally.
Redirect causes browser refresh and new request.

questions.content: What interface provides getRequestDispatcher?

options.feedback: Correct. It creates the dispatcher.
Response doesn't dispatch requests.

questions.content: Which statement is TRUE about Redirect?

options.feedback: Yes. It's a new request initiated by browser.
That describes Forward, not Redirect.

questions.content: When should you use Redirect?

options.feedback: Yes. Avoids duplicate database actions on refresh.
SELECT can be forwarded safely.

questions.content: Sequence the Redirect process:

items.content: Client sends second request to new URL

questions.feedbackCorrect: Correct. Two round trips distinguish Redirect.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Wrong. Browser must receive the instruction first.

questions.content: What separates business logic from UI?

options.feedback: Yes. Model/View/Controller separation.
That mixes everything together.

questions.content: Which component represents business logic?

options.feedback: Correct. Handles data and domain logic.
View displays, Model processes.

questions.content: Which component handles user input?

hints.content: Acts as intermediary between Model and View.

options.feedback: Yes. Receives HTTP requests and routes them.
Model does logic, Controller handles input.

questions.content: Which technology is typically used as View?

hints.content: Can embed Java code in HTML.

options.feedback: Yes. JavaServer Pages for presentation.
Interface defines contract, not display.

questions.content: Does a Servlet require a main() method?

options.feedback: Correct. Container starts it via init().
No. Standard Java apps have main().

questions.content: What implements the Servlet API contract?

hints.content: Defines lifecycle methods like init(), service(), destroy().

options.feedback: Yes. Defines lifecycle methods.
Browser uses the interface but isn't it.

questions.content: GenericServlet is protocol-independent?

options.feedback: Yes. Designed to work with any protocol.
Incorrect. It abstracts protocol specifics.

questions.content: JSP is primarily used for which MVC part?

hints.content: JSP is part of the Presentation Layer.

options.feedback: Correct. Generates dynamic HTML pages.
JSP is for display, not business logic.

questions.content: Where is 'forward' performed?

options.feedback: Yes. Invisible to the client.
Browser thinks it's the original request.

questions.content: If I want to avoid duplicate database inserts, which should I use?

hints.content: Redirect causes a new request, preventing re-submission.

options.feedback: Correct. Browser resets state on reload.
Forward keeps the POST method ready to repeat.

questions.content: What is the relationship between HttpServletRequest and Forward?

hints.content: Request attributes remain available after Forward.

options.feedback: Yes. Attributes stay available in target.
Only lost in Redirect.

questions.content: Which object wraps session information?

options.feedback: Yes. You retrieve session from request.
You write cookies here, but session comes from request.

questions.content: Who calls service() when a request arrives?

hints.content: Container manages the lifecycle and method calls.

options.feedback: Yes. Container orchestrates the thread and method call.
Browser sends request, Container executes service().

knowledgeBase.name: Servlet Container
knowledgeBase.description: Software component managing servlet lifecycle (load, init, service, destroy). Has main program, interacts with Web Server.

knowledgeBase.name: Servlet Lifecycle
knowledgeBase.description: Full process from loading to destruction: Init, Service, Destroy. Managed by container.

knowledgeBase.name: init() method
knowledgeBase.description: Called once after instantiation. Used for one-time setup (DB connection, config).

knowledgeBase.name: service() method
knowledgeBase.description: Core method handling requests. Called by container for each request using threading.

knowledgeBase.name: destroy() method
knowledgeBase.description: Called before unloading. Used to release resources. Prevents memory leaks.

knowledgeBase.name: HttpServlet
knowledgeBase.description: Abstract class implementing service() for HTTP protocols. Provides doGet/doPost scaffolding.

knowledgeBase.name: HttpServletRequest
knowledgeBase.description: Object encapsulating client request info (params, headers, session). Accessed by doGet/doPost.

knowledgeBase.name: HttpServletResponse
knowledgeBase.description: Object used to send responses. Sets headers, types, and writes content back to client.

knowledgeBase.name: RequestDispatcher
knowledgeBase.description: Interface for forwarding/include. Obtained via getRequestDispatcher(). Facilitates internal routing.

knowledgeBase.name: Forward
knowledgeBase.description: Internal server-side dispatch. URL unchanged. Request scope shared. Good for SELECT.

knowledgeBase.name: Redirect
knowledgeBase.description: Client-side navigation. Requires new request. URL changes. Prevents double submit.

knowledgeBase.name: MVC
knowledgeBase.description: Design pattern separating Model (data), View (display), Controller (logic/routing).

knowledgeBase.name: doGet() method
knowledgeBase.description: Handles HTTP GET requests. Used for retrieving data.

knowledgeBase.name: doPost() method
knowledgeBase.description: Handles HTTP POST requests. Used for submitting/changing data.

knowledgeBase.name: GenericServlet
knowledgeBase.description: Protocol-agnostic base class. Implements Servlet/ServicConfig interfaces. Defined init/service/destroy framework.

knowledgeBase.name: Thread
knowledgeBase.description: Container spawns thread per request for service(). Instance shared, thread safe coding required.

knowledgeBase.name: JSP
knowledgeBase.description: JavaServer Pages. Technology for View component. Embeds Java in HTML to produce dynamic output.

knowledgeBase.name: Model
knowledgeBase.description: Encapsulates business logic and data. Independent of View/Controller. Can be reused.

knowledgeBase.name: View
knowledgeBase.description: Responsibility for rendering UI. Uses JSP/HTML. Fetches data from Model.

knowledgeBase.name: Controller
knowledgeBase.description: Receives input, calls Model, selects View. In Web, often implemented as Servlet.

knowledgeBase.name: Servlet (interface)
knowledgeBase.description: Core API interface defining lifecycle methods. Classes must implement or extend it.

evidence.name: Initialization Failure Log
evidence.description: Shows service() called but init() was skipped due to error.

evidence.name: Leaked Database Connection
evidence.description: Connection pool exhausted due to missing destroy() cleanup.

evidence.name: Redirect Chain Analysis
evidence.description: Wireshark capture showing two separate TCP streams for logical single action.

evidence.name: MVC Blueprint
evidence.description: Diagram showing layers clearly defined but coupling broken in Controller.

endings.title: The Root Cause Revealed
endings.content: Cindy admitted using Redirect for SELECT operations unnecessarily, confusing the controller flow. Ben confirmed he missed destroy() to save runtime. Fixing both restores system integrity.
endings.debrief: Players learned: 1) Container manages lifecycle; 2) Service() handles traffic with threads; 3) Forward vs Redirect determines scope; 4) MVC ensures clean separation.
